title: Quadlet: consider supporting environment / .env syntax interpolation
body:
### Issue Description

Environment interpolation does not work in `.env` files set using `EnvironmentFile` option nor in the `Environment` option in  `.container` files.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

`example.container`:

```
[Container]
...
Environment=POSTGRES_USER=example 
Environment=AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=${POSTGRES_USER}
...
```

Nor does it work in an attached `.env` file set using the `EnvironmentFile` option:

```
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mypassword
AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__PASSWORD=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
```

### Describe the results you received

Interpolation does not occur. 

`AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__PASSWORD` and `AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER` are not set.

### Describe the results you expected

`AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER` to be set to `example`
`AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__PASSWORD` to be set to `mypassword`

### podman info output

```yaml
Podman version 5.4.0
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
Not sure that this is a Quadlet issue. I've tried the following:
```
$ podman run --rm -e POSTGRES_USER=example -e AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=${POSTGRES_USER} fedora:42 /bin/bash -c "env"
HOSTNAME=d9168b7254b8
PWD=/
container=oci
HOME=/root
SHLVL=0
POSTGRES_USER=example
PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin
AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=
_=/usr/bin/env
```
You can see that `AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER` is set to nothing.
I've been migrating from a systemd compose.yaml setup and expected this would work. Is interpolation out of the question then?

> systemd compose.yaml setup

Not sure what this means? A systemd unit that runs `docker compose`?

> Is interpolation out of the question then?

Can you please provide an example of what works for you?

From your question, it seems that you're mixing different environment and expecting the same variables to be set. Using `-e POSTGRES_USER=example` in `podman run` sets the environment variable inside the container. So,  `${POSTGRES_USER}` has no meaning outside of it (where the `podman run` call is processed)

> Not sure what this means? A systemd unit that runs docker compose?

I've been running compose files on systemd using `podman-compose systemd -a register` and `systemctl --user enable --now podman-compose@myproject`. These `compose.yaml` files have an associated `.env` file containing the environment variables. These variables include interpolations like:
```
POSTGRES_USER=example 
AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=${POSTGRES_USER}
```

When the compose is ran using systemd the variable `AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER` will take the value of `example` and the compose will run without errors. 

As I've migrated these compose files to quadlet `.container` files and associated them with  `.env` files the interpolation no longer works. Now there is an error during runtime because not all the environment variables got set to the value they should have i.e. `AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER` has the value of ` ` not `example`.

Another simplified example:

```
DOMAIN=example.com
SECURE=secure.${DOMAIN}
AUTH=auth.${DOMAIN}
SHOP=shop.${DOMAIN}
```
I'd like `SHOP` to be set to `shop.example.com` as it does correctly in compose files ran on systemd and not give an error because `SHOP` was set to `shop.`.

I've given simple examples above but some projects can contain complex interpolations. Manually changing each variable to be 'hard' coded would not be practical as these projects can be used in different contexts by simply exporting a different variable.

I hope that's clearer. 

Thank you.

The following is a simple example to demonstrate the situation.

`compose.yaml`:
```
services:
    fedora:
        image: fedora:42
        environment:
            - POSTGRES_USER=example
            - AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=${POSTGRES_USER}
        command: /bin/bash -c "env"

```
Run using `podman-compose systemd -a register` and `systemctl --user enable --now 'podman-compose@myproject'`. `journalctl --user -xeu 'podman-compose@myproject'` will show:

```
...
Sep 11 09:48:41 fedora myproject_fedora_1[40221]: POSTGRES_USER=example
Sep 11 09:48:41 fedora myproject_fedora_1[40221]: AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=example
...
```

That's working as expected. Whereas a quadlet `example.container` file:

```
[Container]
Image=fedora:42
Environment=POSTGRES_USER=example 
Environment=AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=${POSTGRES_USER}
Exec=/bin/bash -c env
```
Run using `systemctl --user start example.service`.  `journalctl --user -xeu example.service` will show:
```
...
Sep 11 09:55:03 fedora systemd-example[41204]: POSTGRES_USER=example
Sep 11 09:55:03 fedora systemd-example[41204]: AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=
...
```

I hope that's clearer.

@orchard0 thanks for clarifying. The environment variables interpolation you are describing is done by `compose` before calling `podman`. 
BTW it is the same for docker:
```
$ docker run --rm -e POSTGRES_USER=example -e AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=${POSTGRES_USER} fedora:42 /bin/bash -c "env"
HOSTNAME=e26afc0695a9
DISTTAG=f42container
PWD=/
FBR=f42
HOME=/root
FGC=f42
SHLVL=0
POSTGRES_USER=example
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER=
_=/usr/sbin/env
``` 

Have you looked into using a K8S yaml and a `.kube` Quadlet?

Variable interpolation on the cli doesn't really make sense since the shell does it already.

For quadlet it just maps to the cli options and systemd does interpolation actually but only for service envs but also not withing several service envs I think. 
Set the base vars for the systemd service section and then you could refer then in the container section most likely as they are just passed as argument on exec start, see https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/25053#discussioncomment-11890601 for example.

Note that I would generally recommend against doing this though since --env cli arg with the "secret" envs would be visable on the cli when the command is run so it will be visible to all the process on the system which can be a security risk.
Using the EnvironmentFile would be better. Note the EnvironmentFile is quite simple and as you noted also doesn't do interpolation, doing so would be a breaking chnage as the file format is kinda locked. Any changes to the parser would likely cause major backwards compatibility issues.

So in general I don't think there is a way to do this with podman.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
And here I was, thinking I'd use quadlet.
This would be very helpful to migrate common compose file patterns to quadlets
